contentHtml:
The air in the room is thick, heavy with unspoken words and the ghosts of conversations past. You can feel it in your chest, a tightness that has nothing to do with the physical space and everything to do with the person who just left. Or maybe they haven’t left at all. Maybe they are miles away, yet their presence lingers, a phantom limb attached to your energetic body. This is the residue of connection, the energetic cords that bind us to people, places, and even past versions of ourselves. We are taught to nurture connections, to hold on, but we are rarely taught the sacred art of letting go. We are not taught how to sever the ties that no longer serve us, the ones that drain our life force and keep us tethered to a past that is a cancelled check.

The Invisible Architecture of Connection

We are constantly building invisible structures around us. Every interaction, every significant relationship, every intense experience creates an energetic cord. Think of it as a bridge of light, a conduit for energy and information. Some of these cords are vibrant and nourishing, pulsing with love, support, and mutual respect. They are the connections that feel expansive, the ones that leave you feeling more yourself. But other cords are dense, sticky, and draining. They are the remnants of toxic relationships, unresolved conflicts, and attachments that have become prisons. These are the cords that need cutting. Your perception changes. You stop trying to pull others into your storm — and you stay in your lane. These cords are not just metaphors; they are palpable realities in the subtle body. They can affect your moods, your thoughts, and even your physical health. Cutting them is not an act of aggression or dismissal; it is an act of profound self-love and spiritual hygiene.

Sensation is information, not entertainment.

Why We Cling to What Hurts

If these cords are so damaging, why do we hold on so tightly? The reasons are as complex and varied as we are. Sometimes, it’s a fear of the unknown, a belief that even a painful connection is better than no connection at all. We mistake the familiar ache for a sign of life. Other times, it’s a misguided sense of loyalty or a deep-seated belief that we are responsible for the feelings of others. We carry the weight of their expectations, their needs, their unresolved pain, believing it is our burden to bear. As the pioneering therapist David Schnarch taught, we often become enmeshed with our partners, losing our sense of self in the process. We forget where we end and they begin. This enmeshment can feel like intimacy, but it is a counterfeit version, a pale imitation of the real thing. Truly — the work is not to create love. You might end up just performing that — instead of really being it. Breaking free from these patterns requires a radical honesty, a willingness to look at the ways we have abandoned ourselves in the name of connection.

Boundaries are not walls. They are membranes.

The Ritual of Release

Energetic cord cutting is a ritual, a sacred act of reclaiming your sovereignty. It is not about banishing someone from your heart, but about reclaiming the energy that has been leaking out. There are many ways to perform this ritual, but the core elements are intention, visualization, and a declaration of freedom. Find a quiet space where you will not be disturbed. Close your eyes and bring to mind the person or situation you wish to cut cords with. Allow yourself to feel the connection, to see the cord that binds you. It may appear as a rope, a chain, or a beam of light. Notice its texture, its color, its weight. Acknowledge the lessons this connection has taught you, the ways it has served you, even in its pain. Then, with a clear and unwavering intention, visualize yourself cutting the cord. You can use a sword of light, a pair of golden scissors, or simply your hand. As you cut the cord, say out loud, “I release you. I release myself. I reclaim my energy. I am free.” Feel the shift in your body as the cord is severed. Feel the energy returning to you, filling you up from the inside out.

What you can feel, you can heal.

Life After the Cut

Cutting an energetic cord is not a one-time event. It is a practice, a daily commitment to your own freedom. In my years of working with clients, I’ve seen how these cords can reattach themselves, especially when the underlying patterns have not been addressed. After a cord-cutting ritual, it is essential to fill the space with new, life-affirming energy. This is a time for radical self-care, for nurturing the connection to your own soul. Spend time in nature, create art, move your body, and surround yourself with people who see and celebrate the real you. Each of us is an ever-changing alchemical equation in pursuit of serenity and balance. This is the work of filling your own cup, of becoming so full of your own light that there is no room for draining connections. It is about learning to be your own source of validation, your own anchor in the storm.

Desire that includes presence becomes devotion.

The Fierce Love of Letting Go

There is a fierceness required in this practice, a willingness to be misunderstood. You don't need to be validated, loved, or understood. You don't need to be peaceful, polished, or perfect. You need to be fucking real. Letting go can feel like a betrayal, especially to those who have benefited from your enmeshment. They may call you selfish, cold, or unloving. They may try to pull you back into the old dynamic. Here, your practice becomes your armor, your fierce commitment to your own liberation. It is a love that says, “I will not abandon myself, not even for you.” It is a love that is willing to stand alone, to face the discomfort of the void, knowing that on the other side is a wholeness that can never be taken away.

Breath is the bridge between the conscious and the unconscious.

The Path of True Connection

Ultimately, the practice of energetic cord cutting is not about isolation, but about creating the space for true connection. When we are no longer leaking our energy to the past, we are free to be fully present in the now. We can engage with others from a place of wholeness, not from a place of need. We can love without attachment, support without enmeshment, and connect without losing ourselves. This is the path of sacred partnership, of conscious intimacy. It is a path that requires courage, honesty, and a willingness to do the deep work of healing. But the reward is a love that is truly liberating, a connection that is a reflection of the divine.

This site provides educational content about conscious intimacy and sacred sexuality. It is not therapy. For sexual trauma, consult a licensed somatic or sex therapist.
faqHtml:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between cutting a cord and ending a relationship?

Cutting an energetic cord is not the same as ending a relationship. You can cut a cord with someone you are still in a relationship with, but with whom you have an unhealthy energetic dynamic. It is about reclaiming your energy and creating healthier boundaries within the connection. In some cases, cutting a cord may lead to the natural end of a relationship, but it is not the primary goal.

How do I know if I need to cut a cord with someone?

Pay attention to how you feel after interacting with someone, or even just thinking about them. If you consistently feel drained, anxious, or resentful, it is a sign that there may be an unhealthy energetic cord. Other signs include obsessive thoughts about the person, an inability to move on from a past relationship, and a feeling of being “stuck.”

Can I cut a cord with a place or a past event?

Yes, you can cut cords with anything that you feel energetically tied to in an unhealthy way. This can include a former home, a past job, or a traumatic event. The process is the same: set your intention, visualize the cord, and make a clear declaration of release. This can be a powerful way to free yourself from the weight of the past and reclaim your power in the present.
